questions.question: Explain the four main components of a computer system model in operating systems.
keyPoints: Hardware - physical components like CPU, memory, I/O devices
Operating System - system software that manages hardware resources
Application Programs - software that performs user tasks
Users - people who interact with the computer system

questions.question: Differentiate between a uniprocessor system and a multiprocessor system.
keyPoints: Uniprocessor has single CPU executing instructions sequentially
Multiprocessor has multiple CPUs sharing memory and I/O devices
Multiprocessor provides increased throughput and reliability
Multiprocessor enables parallel processing

questions.question: What is the difference between preemptive and non-preemptive resources? Give one example of each.
keyPoints: Preemptive resources can be taken away from process without consent
Non-preemptive resources cannot be taken until process releases them
CPU time is preemptive - OS can schedule another process
Printer is non-preemptive - job must complete before others access

questions.question: Describe the process of interrupt handling in a computer system.
keyPoints: Interrupt is a signal requiring immediate CPU attention
CPU suspends current execution and saves its state
Control transfers to interrupt service routine (ISR)
After handling, CPU resumes execution from saved state

questions.question: What is the role of the bootstrap program in the system model?
keyPoints: Stored in ROM or EEPROM on the system
Runs automatically when computer is powered on
Initializes basic hardware components (POST)
Loads operating system kernel into memory and transfers control to it

questions.question: Explain the difference between kernel mode and user mode in operating systems.
keyPoints: Kernel mode (privileged mode) has full access to all hardware and memory
User mode restricts application access for protection and security
System calls provide interface between user and kernel mode
Prevents user programs from directly accessing critical system resources

questions.question: What are the necessary conditions for deadlock to occur in a system?
keyPoints: Mutual Exclusion - only one process can use a resource at a time
No Preemption - resources cannot be forcibly taken away
Hold and Wait - process holds resources while waiting for others
Circular Wait - circular chain of processes each waiting for resource held by next

questions.question: Explain the system model in operating systems with particular emphasis on process management and resource allocation. Include the types of systems and their characteristics.
keyPoints: System model defines structure and components of computer system
Four components: Hardware, OS, Application Programs, Users
Process is program in execution with PCB containing CPU registers, program counter, memory info
Process states: New, Ready, Running, Waiting, Terminated
Types of systems: Uniprocessor, Multiprocessor (SMP, AMP), Distributed
Resource allocation involves managing CPU time, memory, I/O devices
Deadlock prevention/avoidance requires proper resource allocation policies

questions.question: Discuss the boot process in a computer system and the role of various system software components during system startup.
keyPoints: Power-on triggers POST (Power-On Self-Test)
BIOS/UEFI initializes basic hardware components
Bootstrap program stored in ROM loads from MBR/Boot device
Bootloader loads OS kernel into memory
Kernel initializes system components (memory management, process scheduler, device drivers)
User space processes and login managers start
System calls and interrupts become operational for user programs

questions.question: Explain the concept of protection in operating systems. How does the system model ensure protection and security of system resources?
keyPoints: Protection ensures controlled access to system resources
Kernel mode vs User mode provides hardware-level protection
System calls provide controlled interface between user and kernel
Access control lists and permissions regulate resource usage
Memory protection prevents processes from accessing each other's memory
I/O protection restricts direct I/O access to privileged mode
User authentication prevents unauthorized system access
